text: Abstract: Using Generative Pre-Trained Transformer Large Language Models (LLMs) for academic dishonesty has been heavily discussed in the literature lately, along with the need to solve the problem of identifying AI-generated content. This paper discusses the functionalities of the detectors and evaluates them by providing an accuracy and error type analysis to answer the following research questions: (1) Can the existing detectors identify whether the content was produced by a human or by an AI application such as ChatGPT? (2) How do machine translation and content obfuscation affect the identification of AI-generated content? The authors examined 12 publicly available detectors and two commercial detectors, namely Turnitin and PlagiarismCheck. The results showed that none of the existing detectors can reliably identify whether the content is AI-generated or not. The results showed that the detectors have a large number of false negatives and tend to classify the output as human-generated. The authors also found that the performance of the detectors is affected by content obfuscation. The contributions of this paper are threefold. First, it provides a summary of the state-of-the-art work in this area, both in the scientific and non-scientific literature. Second, it provides a summary of one of the most extensive tests done so far based on a rigorous methodology, a large set of original documents, and a large set of detectors. Finally, it highlights the limitations and shortcomings of using the existing detectors in academic institutions.
